symptoms.symptom_name: Acute red, hot, swollen, exquisitely tender upper pinna evolving over hours

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Auricular chondritis produces exactly this picture, but the finding alone is non-specific and shared with bacterial perichondritis.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: acute bacterial perichondritis of the pinna
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Identical early appearance — this is why the presentation must be treated as a possible infection first.
Cartilage-confined inflammation sparing the non-cartilaginous lobe is the classic discriminating sign of auricular chondritis.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: erysipelas / facial cellulitis involving the ear
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Cellulitis/erysipelas involves skin uniformly and does not respect the cartilage boundary, so lobe sparing argues against it.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: acute bacterial perichondritis of the pinna
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Perichondritis can also spare the lobe (it too is cartilage-based), so this is only moderately discriminating and does not by itself exclude infection.
A contained autoimmune flare is typically afebrile; absence of fever lowers the probability of invasive bacterial infection.
Mastoiditis and severe bacterial infection usually produce fever/systemic upset.

symptoms.symptom_name: No purulent ear-canal discharge and no hearing change

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Discharge and hearing change would be expected with otitis externa/media; their absence lowers this probability.

symptoms.symptom_name: No recent ear piercing, trauma or injury

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: acute bacterial perichondritis of the pinna
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Perichondritis usually follows penetrating trauma such as high (cartilage) ear piercing; absence of any trauma lowers its probability.

symptoms.symptom_name: History of prior self-limiting episodes of ear cartilage inflammation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Recurrent episodic chondritis is central to the definition of RELAPSING polychondritis; prior identical episodes strongly favour a flare.

symptoms.symptom_name: Sharp demarcation of inflammation exactly at the cartilage/lobe boundary confirmed on inspection

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: In-person confirmation that inflammation stops exactly where the cartilage ends supports chondritis over uniform cellulitis — a clinician examination finding, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Non-tender mastoid process on deep percussion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A non-tender mastoid on formal percussion argues strongly against mastoiditis — a clinician manoeuvre that cannot be done via chat, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Otoscopy: clear external auditory canal and normal tympanic membrane

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A clear canal and normal drum on otoscopy argue against otitis externa/media — requires an otoscope the patient does not operate, sealed.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: The combination of an acutely inflamed upper pinna that spares the lobe, is afebrile, and recurs episodically is highly characteristic of an auricular relapsing-polychondritis flare.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person ear examination with otoscopy and mastoid palpation
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Face-to-face assessment is required to confirm the cartilage-confined pattern and to exclude bacterial perichondritis, mastoiditis and otitis externa that cannot be safely excluded remotely.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR) and full blood count
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Support assessment of the degree of inflammation and help distinguish a contained flare from invasive bacterial infection; arranged through the in-person reviewer.
Reflexively treating as bacterial infection over-medicalises a likely autoimmune flare; antibiotics are only indicated if in-person assessment supports infection.
Not a first-line telehealth or GP step; reserved for suspected mastoiditis/complications identified on in-person assessment.
